title: Novel vs. Older Regimens: Recurrence & Resistance
text:
Among liver transplant recipients with CRE infection, crude rates of recurrence and emergent resistance look much higher with novel agents than with older regimens. That contrast does not survive proper analysis. Two structural problems make the whole-cohort comparison misleading — novel agents were unavailable before 2018 (a positivity violation), and these outcomes were incompletely recorded in the retrospective era. Restricting to the contemporaneous 2018–2025 era, where both regimens were used and documented identically, and applying inverse-probability-of-treatment weighting (IPTW) with death as a competing risk, novel and older regimens show no detectable difference in either endpoint.






NoteHow to read this page



The valid comparison — the 2018–2025 era IPTW analysis — is presented first. The whole-cohort competing-risks models appear afterward as a secondary, uncorrected analysis, because they are confounded by calendar era and by differential outcome ascertainment (documented below). Death is treated as a competing event throughout.

title: Novel vs. Older Regimens: Recurrence & Resistance
text:
Background
Two distinct threats to validity apply to this question:

Survival bias / competing risk of death. Patients on older regimens die earlier and more often, so they have less opportunity to recur or develop resistance. Treating death as ordinary censoring biases the comparison; the appropriate tools are cause-specific Cox and Fine–Gray subdistribution models.
Calendar era — confounding and ascertainment. Novel agents entered use only from 2018, so a whole-cohort treatment contrast conflates drug with time period. As shown below, the two eras also recorded these outcomes differently.







NoteWhy death is treated as a competing risk



Death is not the outcome here — recurrence and emergent resistance are. But both can only occur in a living patient: once someone dies, they can never subsequently recur or develop resistance. An event that makes the event of interest impossible is, by definition, a competing risk.
Standard survival methods (Kaplan–Meier, ordinary Cox) treat death as ordinary censoring, which assumes the patient is still at risk and could experience the event later. For a patient who has died that assumption is false — their future probability of recurrence is zero, not unknown — so treating deaths as censored overestimates the incidence of recurrence and resistance. This matters acutely here because mortality is much higher in the older-regimen arm (≈48% vs ≈27% in the full cohort): censoring those deaths would inflate that arm’s apparent event risk the most. Modeling death as a competing event (Fine–Gray for cumulative incidence; cause-specific Cox for the rate) is precisely the correction for the survival bias that motivated this analysis.
Note: the competing-risks correction alone does not overturn the crude imbalance — the era restriction and the ascertainment differences below are what explain it.




Show code
raw <- read_sav("CRECOOLT_onlyinfections.sav")

cr <- raw |>
  transmute(
    record_id,
    regimen = factor(if_else(as.numeric(new_drug) == 1, "Novel", "Old"), levels = c("Old", "Novel")),
    study_period = as.numeric(study_period),
    inf_date    = as.Date(cre_infection_date),
    death       = as.numeric(death),
    death_date  = as.Date(death_date),
    dischg_date = as.Date(discharge_date),
    clear_date  = as.Date(clearence_date),
    end_date    = as.Date(end_date),
    rec   = as.numeric(cre_recurrence),
    res   = as.numeric(resistance),
    t_rec = as.numeric(CREinf_to_relapse),
    t_res = as.numeric(CREinf_to_resistance),
    age   = as.numeric(age),
    meld  = as.numeric(meld_score),
    multisite = as.numeric(multisite_colonization),
    sofa  = as.numeric(sofa)
  ) |>
  mutate(
    t_death  = as.numeric(death_date - inf_date),
    # discard discharge dates recorded before infection (data-entry errors),
    # fall back to the latest other valid contact date for censoring
    fu_dischg = if_else(as.numeric(dischg_date - inf_date) >= 0, as.numeric(dischg_date - inf_date), NA_real_),
    fu_clear  = if_else(as.numeric(clear_date  - inf_date) >= 0, as.numeric(clear_date  - inf_date), NA_real_),
    fu_end    = if_else(as.numeric(end_date    - inf_date) >= 0, as.numeric(end_date    - inf_date), NA_real_),
    last_valid = pmax(fu_dischg, fu_clear, fu_end, na.rm = TRUE),
    last_valid = if_else(is.finite(last_valid), last_valid, NA_real_)
  )

# Build (time, status) for a competing-risks endpoint:
#   status 1 = event of interest, 2 = death without event, 0 = censored
make_cr <- function(d, evflag, evtime) {
  d |>
    mutate(
      status = case_when(.data[[evflag]] == 1 ~ 1L, death == 1 ~ 2L, TRUE ~ 0L),
      time = case_when(.data[[evflag]] == 1 ~ .data[[evtime]], death == 1 ~ t_death, TRUE ~ last_valid),
      time = if_else(time == 0, 0.5, time)
    ) |>
    filter(!is.na(time), is.finite(time), time > 0)
}

d_rec <- make_cr(cr, "rec", "t_rec")
d_res <- make_cr(cr, "res", "t_res")


The cohort comprises 405 liver transplant recipients with CRE infection (296 older regimen, 109 novel). Event times come from the dataset’s CREinf_to_relapse and CREinf_to_resistance fields; death timing from death_date. Discharge dates recorded before the infection date (54 records) were treated as invalid, with censoring falling back to the latest valid contact date.

title: Novel vs. Older Regimens: Recurrence & Resistance
section: Why a whole-cohort comparison would mislead
text:
Why a whole-cohort comparison would mislead

Positivity: novel agents only exist from 2018


Show code
table(`Study period` = factor(cr$study_period, 1:2, c("2010–2017", "2018–2025")),
      Regimen = cr$regimen) |>
  as.data.frame.matrix() |>
  rownames_to_column("Study period") |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(title = "Treatment by study period",
             subtitle = "Novel regimens appear only from 2018 — a structural non-overlap")








Treatment by study period


Novel regimens appear only from 2018 — a structural non-overlap


Study period
Old
Novel




2010–2017
222

2018–2025

No patient before 2018 could receive a novel agent, so a whole-cohort contrast is partly a comparison of eras rather than of treatments. A causal comparison must restrict to the period where both regimens were genuinely used.


Differential outcome ascertainment
Before interpreting any cross-era difference, we ask whether recurrence and resistance were recorded the same way in both periods. They were not.


Show code
era_lab <- function(x) factor(as.numeric(x), 1:2, c("2010–2017", "2018–2025"))

raw |>
  mutate(era = era_lab(study_period),
         relapse_date = as.Date(relapse_date),
         new_resistance_date = as.Date(new_resistance_date),
         clear_date = as.Date(clearence_date)) |>
  group_by(era) |>
  summarise(
    N = n(),
    `Recurrence = Yes`      = sprintf("%d (%.1f%%)", sum(cre_recurrence == 1, na.rm = TRUE), 100 * mean(cre_recurrence == 1, na.rm = TRUE)),
    `Resistance = Yes`      = sprintf("%d (%.1f%%)", sum(resistance == 1, na.rm = TRUE), 100 * mean(resistance == 1, na.rm = TRUE)),
    `Flag missing (NA)`     = sum(is.na(cre_recurrence)) + sum(is.na(resistance)),
    `Clearance date`        = sprintf("%d (%.0f%%)", sum(!is.na(clear_date)), 100 * mean(!is.na(clear_date))),
    `Resistance date`       = sum(!is.na(new_resistance_date)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  gt(rowname_col = "era") |>
  tab_source_note(md("*Counts of \"Yes\" outcomes and presence of follow-up dates. Percentages are within-era.*"))




Table 1: Outcome and follow-up documentation by study era. Recurrence and resistance flags carry no missing values in either era — retrospective patients are uniformly coded “No” — yet the supporting follow-up dates are almost entirely absent before 2018.

Counts of “Yes” outcomes and presence of follow-up dates. Percentages are within-era.











Three features mark this as differential ascertainment rather than a true era effect:

Implausible event rates. Recorded recurrence is 1.4% retrospectively vs 19.7% prospectively, and zero resistance events were recorded among 222 retrospective patients.
Uniform “No” coding, not missingness. Neither flag has any NA; every retrospective patient carries an explicit “No”, the signature of a chart abstraction that defaulted absent information to negative.
Follow-up documentation collapses. Not one retrospective patient has a recorded microbiological clearance date — yet recurrence and resistance can only be detected through repeat cultures and follow-up susceptibility testing.







ImportantConsequence for interpretation



The near-zero retrospective rates almost certainly reflect incomplete outcome ascertainment, not a genuine era difference. Older-regimen patients appear event-free partly because their events were not captured. Consequently the whole-cohort crude rates and competing-risks models (shown later) are confounded by ascertainment and should be read as uncorrected. The 2018–2025 within-era IPTW analysis below is the only comparison in which both arms were documented identically.

title: Novel vs. Older Regimens: Recurrence & Resistance
text:
Primary analysis — 2018–2025 era with IPTW

Propensity model and covariate balance


Show code
era2 <- cr |> filter(study_period == 2, !is.na(age)) |>
  mutate(novel = as.integer(regimen == "Novel"))

# Parsimonious propensity model (baseline severity)
ps_fit <- glm(novel ~ age + meld + multisite + sofa, family = binomial, data = era2)
era2$ps <- predict(ps_fit, type = "response")
pT <- mean(era2$novel)
era2$iptw <- ifelse(era2$novel == 1, pT / era2$ps, (1 - pT) / (1 - era2$ps))

# Standardized mean differences before/after weighting
smd <- function(x, g, w = rep(1, length(x))) {
  m1 <- weighted.mean(x[g == 1], w[g == 1]); m0 <- weighted.mean(x[g == 0], w[g == 0])
  v1 <- weighted.mean((x[g == 1] - m1)^2, w[g == 1]); v0 <- weighted.mean((x[g == 0] - m0)^2, w[g == 0])
  (m1 - m0) / sqrt((v1 + v0) / 2)
}
covs <- c("age", "meld", "multisite", "sofa")
tibble(Covariate = covs,
       `SMD (unweighted)` = map_dbl(covs, ~smd(era2[[.x]], era2$novel)),
       `SMD (IPTW)`       = map_dbl(covs, ~smd(era2[[.x]], era2$novel, era2$iptw))) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~round(., 3))) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(title = "Covariate balance",
             subtitle = "Stabilized IPTW; |SMD| < 0.1 indicates good balance")

The N in this era is 182 (73 older, 109 novel). Stabilized weights are well-behaved (range 0.68–1.43) and propensity overlap is complete, so positivity holds within the era.


Weighted competing-risks estimates


Show code
era_fg <- function(d, label, weighted) {
  d$ev <- factor(d$status, 0:2, c("censor", label, "death"))
  fg <- finegray(Surv(time, ev) ~ ., data = d[, c("time", "ev", "regimen", "iptw", "record_id")],
                 etype = label)
  w <- if (weighted) fg$fgwt * fg$iptw else fg$fgwt
  coxph(Surv(fgstart, fgstop, fgstatus) ~ regimen + cluster(record_id),
        weights = w, data = fg, robust = TRUE)
}
e_rec   <- make_cr(era2, "rec", "t_rec")
e_res   <- make_cr(era2, "res", "t_res")
er_recU <- era_fg(e_rec, "recurrence", FALSE)
er_recW <- era_fg(e_rec, "recurrence", TRUE)
er_resU <- era_fg(e_res, "resistance", FALSE)
er_resW <- era_fg(e_res, "resistance", TRUE)


Within the contemporaneous era, the IPTW-weighted Fine–Gray subdistribution hazard ratio (novel vs. older) is 1.03 (0.52–2.05), p=0.930 for CRE recurrence and 1.45 (0.38–5.55), p=0.588 for resistance emergence — neither significant. Weighting changes the estimates only marginally from the unweighted era models (recurrence 1.06 (0.54–2.09), p=0.867; resistance 1.46 (0.39–5.53), p=0.578), because the arms are already well balanced within the era. The decisive correction is the era restriction, not the weighting.


Weighted cumulative incidence


Show code
grid <- 0:120
wcif_band <- function(d, label, nice) {
  d$ev <- factor(d$status, 0:2, c("censor", label, "death"))
  sf <- survfit(Surv(time, ev) ~ regimen, data = d, weights = iptw)
  k <- which(sf$states == label)
  s <- summary(sf, times = grid, extend = TRUE)
  tibble(time = s$time, est = s$pstate[, k], lower = s$lower[, k], upper = s$upper[, k],
         regimen = sub("regimen=", "", as.character(s$strata)), endpoint = nice)
}
band <- bind_rows(
  wcif_band(e_rec, "recurrence", "CRE recurrence"),
  wcif_band(e_res, "resistance", "Resistance emergence")
) |> mutate(regimen = factor(regimen, levels = c("Old", "Novel")))
pt90 <- band |> filter(time == 90)

ggplot(band, aes(time, est, color = regimen, fill = regimen)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper), alpha = 0.15, colour = NA) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 90, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey60", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_errorbar(data = pt90, aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper), width = 4,
                position = position_nudge(x = rep(c(-1.5, 1.5), length.out = nrow(pt90)))) +
  geom_point(data = pt90, size = 2.2,
             position = position_nudge(x = rep(c(-1.5, 1.5), length.out = nrow(pt90)))) +
  facet_wrap(~ endpoint) +
  scale_color_manual(values = pal) + scale_fill_manual(values = pal) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(0, 30, 60, 90, 120)) +
  labs(x = "Days from CRE infection", y = "Cumulative incidence",
       color = "Regimen", fill = "Regimen") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) + theme(legend.position = "top")








Figure 1: IPTW-weighted cumulative incidence of CRE recurrence and resistance emergence within the 2018–2025 era, with death as a competing risk. Shaded bands are 95% confidence intervals; markers show the 90-day estimate with its 95% CI.





The weighted curves overlap for both endpoints, consistent with the null subdistribution hazard ratios. Late-time steps rest on few patients still at risk and should not be over-interpreted.

title: Novel vs. Older Regimens: Recurrence & Resistance
text:
Secondary analysis — full cohort (uncorrected)






WarningThese estimates are confounded



The models in this section pool 2010–2025. They are reported for transparency only. Because novel agents did not exist before 2018 and recurrence/resistance were incompletely ascertained in the retrospective era, the large apparent excess with novel regimens is substantially an artifact of era and ascertainment, not evidence of harm.




Show code
bind_rows(
  d_rec |> count(regimen, status) |> mutate(endpoint = "CRE recurrence"),
  d_res |> count(regimen, status) |> mutate(endpoint = "Resistance emergence")
) |>
  mutate(status = factor(status, 0:2, c("Censored", "Event", "Death (competing)"))) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = status, values_from = n, values_fill = 0) |>
  select(endpoint, regimen, Event, `Death (competing)`, Censored) |>
  gt(groupname_col = "endpoint", rowname_col = "regimen") |>
  tab_header(title = "Crude competing-risks event counts by regimen (full cohort)")

Show code
# Cause-specific Cox: death treated as censoring
cs_rec <- coxph(Surv(time, status == 1) ~ regimen, data = d_rec)
cs_res <- coxph(Surv(time, status == 1) ~ regimen, data = d_res)

# Fine-Gray (unadjusted and multisite-adjusted) via finegray + weighted Cox
fg_fit <- function(d, label, adjust = FALSE) {
  d$ev <- factor(d$status, 0:2, c("censor", label, "death"))
  d$multisite <- factor(if_else(d$multisite == 1, "Yes", "No"), levels = c("No", "Yes"))
  rhs <- if (adjust) c("regimen", "multisite") else "regimen"
  fg <- finegray(reformulate(".", response = "Surv(time, ev)"),
                 data = d[, c("time", "ev", rhs)], etype = label)
  coxph(reformulate(rhs, response = "Surv(fgstart, fgstop, fgstatus)"),
        weights = fgwt, data = fg)
}
fg_rec  <- fg_fit(d_rec, "recurrence")
fg_res  <- fg_fit(d_res, "resistance")
fg_recA <- fg_fit(d_rec, "recurrence", adjust = TRUE)
fg_resA <- fg_fit(d_res, "resistance", adjust = TRUE)


In the pooled cohort the Fine–Gray SHR is 4.58 (2.45–8.56), p<0.001 for recurrence and 7.12 (1.92–26.47), p=0.003 for resistance, and adjustment for multisite colonization barely changes them (4.20 (2.14–8.26), p<0.001 and 7.20 (1.98–26.13), p=0.003). These large “effects” disappear within the contemporaneous era, identifying them as era/ascertainment artifacts.

title: Novel vs. Older Regimens: Recurrence & Resistance
text:
All model variants


Show code
tribble(
  ~Cohort, ~Model, ~`CRE recurrence`, ~`Resistance emergence`,
  "2018–2025 era (valid)", "Fine–Gray SHR, IPTW-weighted", fmt_cox(er_recW), fmt_cox(er_resW),
  "2018–2025 era (valid)", "Fine–Gray SHR, unadjusted",    fmt_cox(er_recU), fmt_cox(er_resU),
  "Full cohort (confounded)", "Fine–Gray SHR, unadjusted",   fmt_cox(fg_rec),  fmt_cox(fg_res),
  "Full cohort (confounded)", "Fine–Gray SHR, + multisite",  fmt_cox(fg_recA), fmt_cox(fg_resA),
  "Full cohort (confounded)", "Cause-specific Cox HR",       fmt_cox(cs_rec),  fmt_cox(cs_res)
) |>
  gt(groupname_col = "Cohort", rowname_col = "Model") |>
  cols_label(`CRE recurrence` = md("**CRE recurrence**<br>Novel vs Old"),
             `Resistance emergence` = md("**Resistance emergence**<br>Novel vs Old")) |>
  tab_footnote("Stabilized IPTW from a propensity model (age, MELD, multisite colonization, SOFA); robust standard errors.",
               locations = cells_stub(rows = "Fine–Gray SHR, IPTW-weighted")) |>

title: Novel vs. Older Regimens: Recurrence & Resistance
text:
Interpretation
After accounting for death as a competing risk and restricting to the contemporaneous era with IPTW, novel agents show no detectable increase in CRE recurrence or resistance. The crude and whole-cohort excess is most consistent with cross-era differences — including demonstrable differential outcome ascertainment in 2010–2017 — rather than a causal effect of the newer drugs.






WarningCaveats




The within-era older-regimen arm is small (events: 12 recurrence, 3 resistance), so the null findings carry wide confidence intervals — evidence against a large harmful effect, not proof of equivalence.
IPTW addresses measured confounding only; residual confounding by indication cannot be excluded.
The ascertainment asymmetry should be confirmed with the study team (were retrospective follow-up cultures and repeat susceptibility testing systematically performed?).



Bottom line. Lead with the 2018–2025 IPTW analysis: novel and older regimens are indistinguishable for recurrence and resistance once death, calendar era, and ascertainment are handled. The headline crude imbalance is an artifact.

title: Diagnostics and Treatment by Study Period
text:
This report compares microbiological diagnostic practices, carbapenem resistance epidemiology, and anti-CRE antibiotic therapy between the retrospective (n = 521) and prospective (n = 171) cohorts of the CRECOOLT study.






NoteScope




Screening, resistance mechanisms, and prophylaxis/management strategy: all colonised or infected patients (retrospective n = 244; prospective n = 86)
Anti-CRE treatment comparisons: CRE-infected patients only (retrospective n = 136; prospective n = 54)





Code
library(tidyverse)
library(patchwork)

# Load data
CRECOOLT_overall <- read_csv("CRECOOLT_overall.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
baseline_data <- CRECOOLT_overall |> filter(is.na(redcap_repeat_instrument))

# ── Cohort definitions ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
colonised_data <- baseline_data |>
  filter(cre_colon == 1 | cre_infection == 1) |>
  mutate(period = factor(ifelse(retro_or_pros == 1, "Retrospective", "Prospective"),
                         levels = c("Retrospective", "Prospective")))

infected_data <- baseline_data |>
  filter(cre_infection == 1) |>
  mutate(period = factor(ifelse(retro_or_pros == 1, "Retrospective", "Prospective"),
                         levels = c("Retrospective", "Prospective")))

# ── Antibiotic exposure data ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Select only required columns before joining to avoid name collisions with
# retro_or_pros / cre_infection in the repeated-instrument rows (which are NA)
period_lkp <- baseline_data |>
  select(record_id, .period = retro_or_pros, .cre_inf = cre_infection)

cre_abx <- CRECOOLT_overall |>
  filter(redcap_repeat_instrument == "exposure_to_cre_antibiotics") |>
  select(record_id, redcap_repeat_instance, drug, combination_treat,
         indication, other_drug) |>
  left_join(period_lkp, by = "record_id") |>
  filter(!is.na(.period)) |>
  mutate(period = factor(ifelse(.period == 1, "Retrospective", "Prospective"),
                         levels = c("Retrospective", "Prospective")))

cre_abx_inf <- cre_abx |> filter(.cre_inf == 1)

# ── Constants and helpers ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
drug_labels <- c(
  "1" = "Ertapenem",        "2"  = "Meropenem",
  "3" = "Amikacin",         "4"  = "Gentamicin",
  "5" = "TMP/SMX",          "6"  = "Tigecycline",
  "7" = "Colistin",         "8"  = "Ceftazidime-avibactam",
  "9" = "Meropenem/vaborbactam", "10" = "Imipenem/relebactam",
  "11" = "Cefiderocol",     "12" = "Fosfomycin",
  "13" = "Other"
)

chi_p <- function(tbl) tryCatch(round(chisq.test(tbl)$p.value, 3), error = function(e) NA_real_)
wrs_p <- function(x, g) tryCatch(round(wilcox.test(x ~ g)$p.value, 3), error = function(e) NA_real_)

retro_n_col <- sum(colonised_data$period == "Retrospective")
pros_n_col  <- sum(colonised_data$period == "Prospective")
retro_n_inf <- sum(infected_data$period  == "Retrospective")
pros_n_inf  <- sum(infected_data$period  == "Prospective")

title: Diagnostics and Treatment by Study Period
text:
This report compares microbiological diagnostic practices, carbapenem resistance epidemiology, and anti-CRE antibiotic therapy between the retrospective (n = 521) and prospective (n = 171) cohorts of the CRECOOLT study.






NoteScope




Screening, resistance mechanisms, and prophylaxis/management strategy: all colonised or infected patients (retrospective n = 244; prospective n = 86)
Anti-CRE treatment comparisons: CRE-infected patients only (retrospective n = 136; prospective n = 54)





Code
library(tidyverse)
library(patchwork)

# Load data
CRECOOLT_overall <- read_csv("CRECOOLT_overall.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
baseline_data <- CRECOOLT_overall |> filter(is.na(redcap_repeat_instrument))

# ── Cohort definitions ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
colonised_data <- baseline_data |>
  filter(cre_colon == 1 | cre_infection == 1) |>
  mutate(period = factor(ifelse(retro_or_pros == 1, "Retrospective", "Prospective"),
                         levels = c("Retrospective", "Prospective")))

infected_data <- baseline_data |>
  filter(cre_infection == 1) |>
  mutate(period = factor(ifelse(retro_or_pros == 1, "Retrospective", "Prospective"),
                         levels = c("Retrospective", "Prospective")))

# ── Antibiotic exposure data ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Select only required columns before joining to avoid name collisions with
# retro_or_pros / cre_infection in the repeated-instrument rows (which are NA)
period_lkp <- baseline_data |>
  select(record_id, .period = retro_or_pros, .cre_inf = cre_infection)

cre_abx <- CRECOOLT_overall |>
  filter(redcap_repeat_instrument == "exposure_to_cre_antibiotics") |>
  select(record_id, redcap_repeat_instance, drug, combination_treat,
         indication, other_drug) |>
  left_join(period_lkp, by = "record_id") |>
  filter(!is.na(.period)) |>
  mutate(period = factor(ifelse(.period == 1, "Retrospective", "Prospective"),
                         levels = c("Retrospective", "Prospective")))

cre_abx_inf <- cre_abx |> filter(.cre_inf == 1)

# ── Constants and helpers ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
drug_labels <- c(
  "1" = "Ertapenem",        "2"  = "Meropenem",
  "3" = "Amikacin",         "4"  = "Gentamicin",
  "5" = "TMP/SMX",          "6"  = "Tigecycline",
  "7" = "Colistin",         "8"  = "Ceftazidime-avibactam",
  "9" = "Meropenem/vaborbactam", "10" = "Imipenem/relebactam",
  "11" = "Cefiderocol",     "12" = "Fosfomycin",
  "13" = "Other"
)

chi_p <- function(tbl) tryCatch(round(chisq.test(tbl)$p.value, 3), error = function(e) NA_real_)
wrs_p <- function(x, g) tryCatch(round(wilcox.test(x ~ g)$p.value, 3), error = function(e) NA_real_)

retro_n_col <- sum(colonised_data$period == "Retrospective")
pros_n_col  <- sum(colonised_data$period == "Prospective")
retro_n_inf <- sum(infected_data$period  == "Retrospective")
pros_n_inf  <- sum(infected_data$period  == "Prospective")

Anti-CRE drug use by study period (infected patients)




Key findings:

Ceftazidime-avibactam use increased markedly (55.9% → 77.8%, p = 0.008), reflecting its emergence as the preferred backbone agent for KPC-producers
Colistin use declined substantially (34.6% → 11.1%, p = 0.002), consistent with the shift away from polymyxin-based regimens given their toxicity profile
Fosfomycin use also declined (12.5% → 1.9%, p = 0.047), likely replaced by newer β-lactam/β-lactamase inhibitor combinations
Meropenem remained in common use in both periods (~68% vs 63%), typically as part of combination regimens or for susceptible isolates

text:
Survival Analysis by CRE Therapy and Resistance Mechanism
Kaplan-Meier survival curves from time of CRE infection to death, censored at hospital discharge or 90 days. Patients are classified by:

Therapy group: any exposure to ceftazidime-avibactam, meropenem-vaborbactam, or cefiderocol (“Novel BL/BLI”) vs other agents
Carbapenemase mechanism: KPC vs Non-KPC (OXA-48, NDM, VIM, or other)

library(survival)

# ── JAMA-style theme ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

    axis.text          = element_text(size = 9),

    legend.text        = element_text(size = 9),
    legend.position    = "top",
    legend.key.width   = unit(1.2, "cm"),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_line(colour = "grey90", linewidth = 0.3),
    strip.background   = element_blank(),
    strip.text         = element_text(face = "bold")
  )

# ── Therapy classification per patient ───────────────────────────────────────
# Novel BL/BLI = any exposure to ceftazidime-avibactam (8),
#                meropenem-vaborbactam (9), or cefiderocol (11)
therapy_per_patient <- cre_abx_inf |>
  group_by(record_id) |>
  summarise(
    new_therapy  = any(drug %in% c(8, 9, 11), na.rm = TRUE),
    combination  = any(combination_treat == 1, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# ── Survival dataset ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
surv_infected <- infected_data |>
  left_join(therapy_per_patient, by = "record_id") |>
  mutate(
    surv_time = case_when(
      !is.na(death_date) & !is.na(cre_infection_date) ~
        as.numeric(death_date - cre_infection_date),
      !is.na(discharge_date) & !is.na(cre_infection_date) ~
        as.numeric(discharge_date - cre_infection_date),
      TRUE ~ NA_real_
    ),
    surv_event = ifelse(death == 1, 1, 0),
    surv_time  = ifelse(surv_time < 0, NA_real_, surv_time),

    therapy_group = factor(
      ifelse(new_therapy, "Novel BL/BLI", "Other therapies"),
      levels = c("Novel BL/BLI", "Other therapies")
    ),
    combo_group = factor(
      case_when(
        new_therapy    ~ "Novel BL/BLI",
        combination    ~ "Combination (other agents)",
        TRUE           ~ "Monotherapy (other agents)"
      ),
      levels = c("Novel BL/BLI", "Combination (other agents)",
                 "Monotherapy (other agents)")
    ),
    carbapenemase = case_when(
      cre_isolate___1 == 1 ~ "KPC",
      cre_isolate___2 == 1 ~ "OXA-48",
      cre_isolate___3 == 1 ~ "VIM",
      cre_isolate___4 == 1 ~ "NDM",
      cre_isolate___5 == 1 ~ "Other/Unknown",
      TRUE                 ~ "Not typed"
    ),
    carba_group = factor(
      ifelse(carbapenemase == "KPC",
             "KPC",
             "Non-KPC (OXA-48/NDM/VIM/Other)"),
      levels = c("KPC", "Non-KPC (OXA-48/NDM/VIM/Other)")
    )
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(surv_time), surv_time >= 0, !is.na(surv_event))

# ── Cox model dataset and fit (built here so all downstream chunks can use it)
cox_data <- surv_infected |>
  mutate(
    infect_src = factor(
      case_when(
        infection_source___1 == 1                              ~ "Bloodstream",
        infection_source___2 == 1                              ~ "Respiratory",
        infection_source___3 == 1                              ~ "Urinary",
        infection_source___4 == 1 | infection_source___5 == 1 ~ "Surgical/Abd",
        infection_source___6 == 1                              ~ "Other",
        TRUE                                                   ~ NA_character_
      ),
      levels = c("Bloodstream","Respiratory","Urinary","Surgical/Abd","Other")
    ),
    therapy_fac = relevel(factor(therapy_group), ref = "Other therapies"),
    period_fac  = relevel(factor(period),         ref = "Retrospective"),
    meld_s      = as.numeric(scale(meld_score)),
    age_s       = as.numeric(scale(age))
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(meld_score), !is.na(infect_src))

cox_fit <- coxph(
  Surv(surv_time, surv_event) ~
    therapy_fac + period_fac + meld_s + age_s + infect_src,
  data = cox_data
)

title: Diagnostics and Treatment by Study Period
text:
Unadjusted Survival by Study Period
Kaplan-Meier curves comparing overall survival from time of CRE infection between the retrospective and prospective cohorts. This contextualises the therapy-effect curves above by showing the underlying difference in outcomes across the two study periods.


Code
library(patchwork)

pal_period <- c("Retrospective" = "#374E55", "Prospective" = "#DF8F44")

# ── KM fit ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
fit_period <- survfit(Surv(surv_time, surv_event) ~ period,
                      data = surv_infected)

# ── Tidy to long form ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
km_df <- bind_rows(
  tibble(time = 0, surv = 1, lower = 1, upper = 1,
         period = factor(c("Retrospective", "Prospective"),
                         levels = c("Retrospective", "Prospective"))),
  tibble(
    time   = fit_period$time,
    surv   = fit_period$surv,
    lower  = fit_period$lower,
    upper  = fit_period$upper,
    period = factor(
      rep(c("Retrospective", "Prospective"), fit_period$strata),
      levels = c("Retrospective", "Prospective")
    )
  )
)

# ── Risk table ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
risk_period <- map_df(seq(0, 90, 15), function(t) {
  surv_infected |>
    group_by(period) |>
    summarise(at_risk = sum(surv_time >= t, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>
    mutate(time = t)
})

# ── Log-rank p-value ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
lr      <- survdiff(Surv(surv_time, surv_event) ~ period, data = surv_infected)
lr_pval <- 1 - pchisq(lr$chisq, df = 1)
p_label <- ifelse(lr_pval < 0.001, "p < 0.001",
            ifelse(lr_pval < 0.01,  sprintf("p = %.3f", lr_pval),
                                    sprintf("p = %.2f",  lr_pval)))

# ── 90-day endpoint labels ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
surv_90_p <- km_df |>
  filter(time <= 90) |>
  group_by(period) |>
  slice_tail(n = 1) |>
  mutate(label = paste0(round(surv * 100, 1), "%"))

# ── Main curve ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
p_period_km <- ggplot(km_df, aes(x = time, y = surv * 100,
                                  colour = period, fill = period)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lower * 100, ymax = upper * 100),
              alpha = 0.12, colour = NA) +
  geom_step(linewidth = 0.85) +
  geom_text(data = surv_90_p,
            aes(x = 92, y = surv * 100, label = label, colour = period),
            hjust = 0, vjust = -0.7, size = 3.2, fontface = "bold",
            show.legend = FALSE) +
  annotate("text", x = 3, y = 18, label = p_label,
           hjust = 0, size = 3.5, colour = "grey25") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 50, linetype = "dotted",
             colour = "grey70", linewidth = 0.4) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = pal_period, name = NULL) +
  scale_fill_manual(values   = pal_period, name = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 100), breaks = seq(0, 90, 15),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.01))) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 105), breaks = seq(0, 100, 25),
                     labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%"),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0))) +
  labs(
    title    = "Unadjusted Survival by Study Period",
    subtitle = "CRE-infected patients — retrospective vs prospective cohort",
    x = "Days from CRE Infection",
    y = "Survival Probability"
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title         = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13),
    plot.subtitle      = element_text(size = 9, colour = "grey40"),
    legend.position    = c(0.22, 0.18),
    legend.background  = element_rect(fill = alpha("white", 0.8), colour = NA),
    legend.key.width   = unit(1.4, "cm"),
    legend.text        = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.text          = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title         = element_text(size = 10),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_line(colour = "grey92", linewidth = 0.3),
    plot.margin        = margin(8, 60, 0, 8)
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(clip = "off")

# ── Risk table ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
p_risk_period <- ggplot(risk_period,
                         aes(x = time, y = fct_rev(period), label = at_risk)) +
  geom_text(aes(colour = period), size = 3.2) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = pal_period, guide = "none") +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 100), breaks = seq(0, 90, 15)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL, title = "No. at risk") +
  theme_classic(base_size = 10) +
  theme(
    plot.title  = element_text(size = 9, face = "bold", colour = "grey30",
                               margin = margin(b = 2)),
    axis.text.x = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks  = element_blank(),
    axis.line   = element_blank(),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 9),
    panel.grid  = element_blank(),
    plot.margin = margin(0, 60, 4, 8)
  )

p_period_km / p_risk_period + plot_layout(heights = c(5, 1))





Unadjusted Kaplan-Meier survival curves by study period (retrospective vs prospective cohort). Shaded bands = 95% confidence intervals. Percentages at right = unadjusted 90-day survival. Log-rank p-value shown.










Note



The prospective cohort has markedly worse unadjusted survival (90-day: 20.1% vs 50.9%). This likely reflects both shorter follow-up time in the prospective cohort and a higher proportion of patients with novel BL/BLI therapy (which, despite better outcomes, is enriched among more severely ill patients referred during the prospective period). The adjusted curves below account for these differences.



Adjusted Survival by Study Period
Survival curves from the full Cox model holding therapy group (other therapies), MELD score, and infection source at their reference/mean values — isolating the independent effect of study period on survival.


Code
# cox_data and cox_fit built in km-setup chunk

# ── Newdata: two rows varying only period ─────────────────────────────────────
covariate_means_period <- cox_data |>
  summarise(
    meld_s      = mean(meld_s,  na.rm = TRUE),
    age_s       = mean(age_s,   na.rm = TRUE),
    therapy_fac = "Other therapies",
    infect_src  = "Bloodstream"
  )

newdata_period <- bind_rows(
  covariate_means_period |> mutate(period_fac = "Retrospective"),
  covariate_means_period |> mutate(period_fac = "Prospective")
) |>
  mutate(
    therapy_fac = factor(therapy_fac,
                         levels = c("Other therapies", "Novel BL/BLI")),
    period_fac  = factor(period_fac,
                         levels = c("Retrospective", "Prospective")),
    infect_src  = factor(infect_src,
                         levels = c("Bloodstream","Respiratory","Urinary",
                                    "Surgical/Abd","Other"))
  )

# ── Adjusted survfit ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
adj_period_fit <- survfit(cox_fit, newdata = newdata_period)

adj_period_df <- bind_rows(
  tibble(time = 0, surv = 1, lower = 1, upper = 1,
         period = factor(c("Retrospective","Prospective"),
                         levels = c("Retrospective","Prospective"))),
  map_df(1:2, function(i) {
    tibble(
      time   = adj_period_fit$time,
      surv   = adj_period_fit$surv[, i],
      lower  = adj_period_fit$lower[, i],
      upper  = adj_period_fit$upper[, i],
      period = c("Retrospective","Prospective")[i]
    )
  }) |>
    mutate(period = factor(period, levels = c("Retrospective","Prospective")))
)

# ── 90-day endpoint labels ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
surv_90_adj_p <- adj_period_df |>
  filter(time <= 90) |>
  group_by(period) |>
  slice_tail(n = 1) |>
  mutate(label = paste0(round(surv * 100, 1), "%"))

pal_period <- c("Retrospective" = "#374E55", "Prospective" = "#DF8F44")

# ── Main curve ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
p_adj_period_km <- ggplot(adj_period_df,
                           aes(x = time, y = surv * 100,
                               colour = period, fill = period)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lower * 100, ymax = upper * 100),
              alpha = 0.12, colour = NA) +
  geom_step(linewidth = 0.85) +
  geom_text(data = surv_90_adj_p,
            aes(x = 92, y = surv * 100, label = label, colour = period),
            hjust = 0, vjust = -0.7, size = 3.2, fontface = "bold",
            show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 50, linetype = "dotted",
             colour = "grey70", linewidth = 0.4) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = pal_period, name = NULL) +
  scale_fill_manual(values   = pal_period, name = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 100), breaks = seq(0, 90, 15),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.01))) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 105), breaks = seq(0, 100, 25),
                     labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%"),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0))) +
  labs(
    title    = "Adjusted Survival by Study Period",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "Cox model adjusted for therapy group, MELD score, age, and infection source\n",
      "Covariates held at: other therapies (reference), mean MELD & age, bloodstream source"
    ),
    x = "Days from CRE Infection",
    y = "Survival Probability"
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title         = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13),
    plot.subtitle      = element_text(size = 9, colour = "grey40",
                                      lineheight = 1.3),
    legend.position    = c(0.22, 0.18),
    legend.background  = element_rect(fill = alpha("white", 0.8), colour = NA),
    legend.key.width   = unit(1.4, "cm"),
    legend.text        = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.text          = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title         = element_text(size = 10),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_line(colour = "grey92", linewidth = 0.3),
    plot.margin        = margin(8, 60, 0, 8)
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(clip = "off")

# ── Risk table (observed counts) ─────────────────────────────────────────────
p_risk_adj_p <- ggplot(risk_period,
                        aes(x = time, y = fct_rev(period), label = at_risk)) +
  geom_text(aes(colour = period), size = 3.2) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = pal_period, guide = "none") +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 100), breaks = seq(0, 90, 15)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL, title = "No. at risk") +
  theme_classic(base_size = 10) +
  theme(
    plot.title  = element_text(size = 9, face = "bold", colour = "grey30",
                               margin = margin(b = 2)),
    axis.text.x = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks  = element_blank(),
    axis.line   = element_blank(),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 9),
    panel.grid  = element_blank(),
    plot.margin = margin(0, 60, 4, 8)
  )

p_adj_period_km / p_risk_adj_p + plot_layout(heights = c(5, 1))





Adjusted survival curves by study period from Cox proportional hazards model. Covariates held at: other therapies (reference), mean MELD score, mean age, bloodstream infection source. Shaded bands = 95% confidence intervals.










WarningInterpreting the adjusted period curves



After adjusting for therapy type, MELD score, age, and infection source, the prospective period still shows substantially lower survival (90-day: 9.5% vs 33.3%). This suggests the period effect is not fully explained by the measured covariates — unmeasured factors such as patient frailty, post-transplant complications, or centre-level changes in practice may contribute. The wide confidence interval for the prospective group reflects the smaller sample size (n = 46).

title: Diagnostics and Treatment by Study Period
text:
Adjusted Cox Proportional Hazards Model
To account for potential confounding by study period, disease severity, age, and infection source, a multivariable Cox model was fitted with therapy group (Novel BL/BLI vs other) as the primary exposure.


Code
# cox_data and cox_fit are built in km-setup; here we just extract the HR table

# ── Extract HR table ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
cox_tbl <- broom::tidy(cox_fit, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE) |>
  mutate(
    label = recode(term,
      "therapy_facNovel BL/BLI"    = "Novel BL/BLI vs Other",
      "period_facProspective"      = "Prospective vs Retrospective",
      "meld_s"                     = "MELD score (per SD)",
      "age_s"                      = "Age (per SD)",
      "infect_srcRespiratory"      = "Respiratory vs Bloodstream",
      "infect_srcUrinary"          = "Urinary vs Bloodstream",
      "infect_srcSurgical/Abd"     = "Surgical/Abd vs Bloodstream",
      "infect_srcOther"            = "Other source vs Bloodstream"
    ),
    sig = ifelse(p.value < 0.05, "*", "")
  ) |>
  select(label, HR = estimate, `95% CI lower` = conf.low,
         `95% CI upper` = conf.high, `p value` = p.value)

knitr::kable(cox_tbl, digits = c(0, 2, 2, 2, 3),
  caption = paste0("Adjusted Cox proportional hazards model: all-cause mortality ",
                   "from CRE infection (n = ", nrow(cox_data),

Forest Plot


Code
forest_data <- cox_tbl |>
  rename(HR = HR, lower = `95% CI lower`, upper = `95% CI upper`, p = `p value`) |>
  mutate(
    label = factor(label, levels = rev(label)),
    group = case_when(
      grepl("Novel|Prospective", label) ~ "Primary",
      grepl("MELD|Age", label)          ~ "Continuous",
      TRUE                               ~ "Infection source"
    ),
    sig     = p < 0.05,
    hr_text = sprintf("%.2f (%.2f\u2013%.2f)", HR, lower, upper),
    p_text  = ifelse(p < 0.001, "<0.001",
               ifelse(p < 0.01,  sprintf("%.3f", p),
                                  sprintf("%.2f",  p)))
  )

ggplot(forest_data, aes(x = HR, y = label)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50",
             linewidth = 0.5) +

                orientation = "y", width = 0.25, linewidth = 0.7) +

            hjust = 1, size = 3.0, family = "mono") +

Forest plot of adjusted hazard ratios from Cox proportional hazards model






Adjusted Survival Curves
Survival curves derived from the fitted Cox model with all covariates held at their reference or mean values, varying only therapy group. This removes the confounding effects of study period, disease severity, age, and infection source, giving a cleaner estimate of the therapy effect on survival.


Code
# ── Covariate grid: mean continuous variables, reference-level factors ─────
covariate_means <- cox_data |>
  summarise(
    meld_s     = mean(meld_s,  na.rm = TRUE),
    age_s      = mean(age_s,   na.rm = TRUE),
    period_fac = "Retrospective",
    infect_src = "Bloodstream"
  )

newdata_adj <- bind_rows(
  covariate_means |> mutate(therapy_fac = "Novel BL/BLI"),
  covariate_means |> mutate(therapy_fac = "Other therapies")
) |>
  mutate(
    therapy_fac = factor(therapy_fac,
                         levels = c("Other therapies", "Novel BL/BLI")),
    period_fac  = factor(period_fac,
                         levels = c("Retrospective", "Prospective")),
    infect_src  = factor(infect_src,
                         levels = c("Bloodstream", "Respiratory", "Urinary",
                                    "Surgical/Abd", "Other"))
  )

# ── Adjusted survfit ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
adj_fit <- survfit(cox_fit, newdata = newdata_adj)

tidy_adj <- function(sf, labels) {
  map_df(seq_along(labels), function(i) {
    tibble(time  = sf$time,
           surv  = sf$surv[, i],
           lower = sf$lower[, i],
           upper = sf$upper[, i],
           group = labels[i])
  }) |> mutate(group = factor(group, levels = labels))
}

adj_df <- bind_rows(
  tibble(time = 0, surv = 1, lower = 1, upper = 1,
         group = factor(c("Novel BL/BLI", "Other therapies"),
                        levels = c("Novel BL/BLI", "Other therapies"))),
  tidy_adj(adj_fit, c("Novel BL/BLI", "Other therapies"))
)

# ── Risk table (observed counts) ─────────────────────────────────────────────
risk_tbl <- map_df(seq(0, 90, 15), function(t) {
  surv_infected |>
    group_by(therapy_group) |>
    summarise(at_risk = sum(surv_time >= t, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>
    mutate(time = t)
}) |>
  mutate(therapy_group = factor(therapy_group,
                                levels = c("Novel BL/BLI", "Other therapies")))

# ── 90-day survival labels ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
surv_90 <- adj_df |>
  filter(time <= 90) |>
  group_by(group) |>
  slice_tail(n = 1) |>
  mutate(label = paste0(round(surv * 100, 1), "%"))

pal <- c("Novel BL/BLI" = "#374E55", "Other therapies" = "#DF8F44")

# ── Main curve ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
p_adj_km <- ggplot(adj_df, aes(x = time, y = surv * 100,
                                colour = group, fill = group)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lower * 100, ymax = upper * 100),
              alpha = 0.12, colour = NA) +
  geom_step(linewidth = 0.85) +
  geom_text(data = surv_90,
            aes(x = 92, y = surv * 100, label = label, colour = group),
            hjust = 0, vjust = -0.7, size = 3.2, fontface = "bold",
            show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 50, linetype = "dotted",
             colour = "grey70", linewidth = 0.4) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = pal, name = NULL) +
  scale_fill_manual(values   = pal, name = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits  = c(0, 100), breaks = seq(0, 90, 15),
                     expand  = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.01))) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits  = c(0, 105), breaks = seq(0, 100, 25),
                     labels  = function(x) paste0(x, "%"),
                     expand  = expansion(mult = c(0, 0))) +
  labs(
    title    = "Adjusted Survival Curves — Novel BL/BLI vs Other Therapies",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "Cox model adjusted for period, MELD score, age, and infection source\n",
      "Covariates held at: retrospective period, mean MELD & age, bloodstream source"
    ),
    x = "Days from CRE Infection",
    y = "Survival Probability"
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title         = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13),
    plot.subtitle      = element_text(size = 9, colour = "grey40",
                                      lineheight = 1.3),
    legend.position    = c(0.22, 0.18),
    legend.background  = element_rect(fill = alpha("white", 0.8), colour = NA),
    legend.key.width   = unit(1.4, "cm"),
    legend.text        = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.text          = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title         = element_text(size = 10),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_line(colour = "grey92", linewidth = 0.3),
    plot.margin        = margin(8, 60, 0, 8)
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(clip = "off")

# ── Risk table ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
p_risk <- ggplot(risk_tbl,
                 aes(x = time, y = fct_rev(therapy_group), label = at_risk)) +
  geom_text(aes(colour = therapy_group), size = 3.2) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = pal, guide = "none") +
  scale_x_continuous(limits  = c(0, 100), breaks = seq(0, 90, 15)) +
  scale_y_discrete(labels = c("Other therapies" = "Other",

Adjusted survival curves from Cox proportional hazards model. Covariates held at: retrospective period, mean MELD score, mean age, bloodstream infection source. Shaded bands = 95% confidence intervals. Percentages at right = adjusted 90-day survival.






Proportional Hazards Assumption






NoteSchoenfeld Residuals Test — click to expand





The proportional hazards (PH) assumption was tested for each covariate using scaled Schoenfeld residuals (cox.zph()). A non-significant p-value and approximately flat smooth curve indicate the assumption is satisfied.


Code
ph_test <- cox.zph(cox_fit)

# Formal test table
ph_tbl <- as.data.frame(ph_test$table) |>
  tibble::rownames_to_column("Covariate") |>
  mutate(
    Covariate = recode(Covariate,
      "therapy_fac"  = "Novel BL/BLI vs Other",
      "period_fac"   = "Prospective vs Retrospective",
      "meld_s"       = "MELD score (per SD)",
      "age_s"        = "Age (per SD)",
      "infect_src"   = "Infection source",
      "GLOBAL"       = "GLOBAL"
    ),
    Verdict = case_when(
      Covariate == "GLOBAL" ~ ifelse(p < 0.05, "⚠ Violated",  "✅ Satisfied"),
      TRUE                  ~ ifelse(p < 0.05, "⚠ Violated",  "✅ Satisfied")
    )
  ) |>
  rename(`χ²` = chisq, df = df, `p value` = p)

knitr::kable(ph_tbl, digits = c(0, 3, 0, 3, 0),
  caption = "Schoenfeld residuals test for proportional hazards assumption") |>
  kableExtra::row_spec(nrow(ph_tbl), bold = TRUE)

ph_gg <- ggcoxzph(ph_test,
  var     = c("therapy_fac", "period_fac", "meld_s", "age_s", "infect_src"),
  ggtheme = theme_classic(base_size = 10) +
    theme(
      plot.title         = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10),
      axis.text          = element_text(size = 8, colour = "grey30"),
      axis.title         = element_text(size = 9),
      panel.grid.major.y = element_line(colour = "grey92", linewidth = 0.3)
    )
)

clean_labels <- c(
  "Novel BL/BLI vs Other  (p = 0.241)",
  "Prospective vs Retro  (p = 0.491)",
  "MELD score  (p = 0.196)",
  "Age  (p = 0.949)",
  "Infection source  (p = 0.079)"
)

for (i in seq_along(ph_gg)) {
  ph_gg[[i]] <- ph_gg[[i]] +
    labs(title = clean_labels[i], x = "Time (days)", y = NULL) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed",
               colour = "grey50", linewidth = 0.4)
}

patchwork::wrap_plots(ph_gg, ncol = 2) +
  patchwork::plot_annotation(
    title    = "Proportional Hazards Assumption — Scaled Schoenfeld Residuals",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "Global test: \u03c7\u00b2 = ",
      round(ph_test$table["GLOBAL", "chisq"], 2),
      "  df = ", ph_test$table["GLOBAL", "df"],
      "  p = ", round(ph_test$table["GLOBAL", "p"], 3),
      "  |  Smooth line should be approximately horizontal if PH holds"
    ),
    theme = theme(
      plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 9, colour = "grey40")
    )
  ) &
  theme(axis.title.y = element_blank())





Scaled Schoenfeld residuals by covariate. Approximately flat smooth curves indicate the PH assumption is satisfied.




Interpretation: The PH assumption is satisfied for all covariates individually and globally (global p = 0.205). The infection source term is the weakest (p = 0.079) but remains non-significant. No time-varying coefficients or stratification are required.









WarningResidual Confounding



The prospective cohort period and novel BL/BLI use are correlated by design (novel agents became available over time). Even after adjustment, the HR for novel BL/BLI (HR 0.38, 95% CI 0.21–0.70) and the HR for prospective period both remain significant. This collinearity should be interpreted with caution; the two effects may not be fully separable in this dataset.

title: Validation cohort
text:
Descriptive analysis of cumulative incidence of censoring, hospital discharge, CRE infection or death. A competing risk (Fine-Gray) model was fitted to outcome of death, hospital discharge, or CRE infection censored to day 180. The cumulative incidence of competing risks are shown in Figure 1.

Code
# -------------------------------------------------
# 1. Unadjusted Fine–Gray (CRE infection as event)
# -------------------------------------------------
library(prodlim)
library(riskRegression)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library (here)


library(here)

# Load the exported dataset
data_extract <- read.csv(here("data", "data_extract.csv"), stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

# Quick check

set.seed(123)   # for reproducibility

# ----------------------------
# 1. Prepare data
# ----------------------------
df_model <- data.frame(
  time_c = as.numeric(data_extract$time_c),
  outcome_comp = as.integer(data_extract$outcome_comp)
) %>%
  filter(!is.na(time_c) & time_c >= 0)

# ----------------------------
# 2. Base CIF fit
# ----------------------------
ci_fit <- prodlim(Hist(time_c, outcome_comp) ~ 1, data = df_model)

# ----------------------------
# 3. Times to evaluate
# ----------------------------
times_seq <- seq(0, 180, by = 5)

# ----------------------------
# 4. Bootstrap CIF estimates
# ----------------------------
B <- 200   # number of bootstrap resamples (increase to 1000 for publication)

boot_cif <- function(data, times, cause) {
  fit <- prodlim(Hist(time_c, outcome_comp) ~ 1, data = data)
  predict(fit, cause = cause, times = times)
}

boot_results <- lapply(1:3, function(cause) {
  mat <- replicate(B, {
    idx <- sample(nrow(df_model), replace = TRUE)
    boot_data <- df_model[idx, ]
    boot_cif(boot_data, times_seq, cause)
  })
  # mean and CI across bootstrap runs
  est <- predict(ci_fit, cause = cause, times = times_seq)
  lower <- apply(mat, 1, quantile, 0.025, na.rm = TRUE)
  upper <- apply(mat, 1, quantile, 0.975, na.rm = TRUE)
  data.frame(times = times_seq, CIF = est, lower = lower, upper = upper,
             cause = factor(cause))
})

ci_tidy <- bind_rows(boot_results) %>%
  mutate(cause = recode(cause,
                        "1" = "CRE infection",
                        "2" = "Discharge",
                        "3" = "Death"))

# ----------------------------
# 5. NEJM-style plot
# ----------------------------
nejm_colors <- c("CRE infection" = "#D81B60",
                 "Discharge"     = "#1E88E5",
                 "Death"         = "black")

p_nejm <- ggplot(ci_tidy, aes(x = times, y = CIF, color = cause, fill = cause)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper), alpha = 0.15, color = NA) +
  geom_line(size = 1.2) +
  labs(x = "Days since OLT",
       y = "Cumulative incidence",
       color = NULL, fill = NULL) +
  scale_color_manual(values = nejm_colors) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = nejm_colors) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "top",
    legend.title = element_blank(),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 12),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 12),
    axis.ticks.length = unit(0.2, "cm")
  )

print(p_nejm)















Figure 1: Cumulative risk of competing outcomes in combined retrospective and prospective study cohorts



A competing risk multivariate, Fine-gray model for day+180 CRE infection was then fitted using the original CRECOOLT covariate from the score:

Surgical re intervention after OLT
Need for mechanical ventilation
Acute renal failure after transplant
CRE colonization prior to OLT
CRE colonization after OLT
KPC-carbapenemase producing organism







NoteClarification from previous analysis



In this initial analysis I have not yet created a new variable limiting CRE colonization to within 60 days pretransplant.


Predicted cumulative incidence of CRE infection versus the observed for the total (retrospective plus prospective cohort) is shown in Figure 2.

Code
#############################################
## Multivariable Fine–Gray + Calibration Plot
## with Jittered Dots + Legend for Outcomes
#############################################

library(dplyr)
library(cmprsk)
library(riskRegression)
library(prodlim)
library(ggplot2)

# ============================================================
# 1. Prepare dataset with covariates
# ============================================================
df_model <- data_extract %>%
  mutate(
    post_olt_compli1 = ifelse(post_olt_compli___1 == 1, 1, 0),
    post_olt_compli3 = ifelse(post_olt_compli___3 == 1, 1, 0),
    post_olt_compli5 = ifelse(post_olt_compli___5 == 1, 1, 0),
    multisite_col    = ifelse(multisite_colonization == 1, 1, 0),
    cre_col1         = ifelse(cre_colonization___1 == 1, 1, 0),
    cre_col2         = ifelse(cre_colonization___2 == 1, 1, 0)
  ) %>%
  select(time_c, outcome_comp,
         post_olt_compli1, post_olt_compli3, post_olt_compli5,
         multisite_col, cre_col1, cre_col2) %>%
  filter(!is.na(time_c) & time_c >= 0)

# ============================================================
# 2. Fit Fine–Gray model (cause = CRE infection)
# ============================================================
fg_risk <- FGR(
  Hist(time_c, outcome_comp) ~ 
    post_olt_compli1 + post_olt_compli3 + post_olt_compli5 +
    multisite_col + cre_col1 + cre_col2,
  data  = df_model,
  cause = 1
)

# ============================================================
# 3. Predictions vs Observed CIF
# ============================================================
times_seq <- seq(0, 180, by = 5)

pred_cif <- predict(
  fg_risk,
  newdata = df_model,
  cause = 1,
  times = times_seq,
  se = FALSE,
  keep.newdata = FALSE
)

pred_df <- data.frame(
  times = times_seq,
  CIF_pred = colMeans(pred_cif, na.rm = TRUE)
)

ci_fit <- prodlim(Hist(time_c, outcome_comp) ~ 1, data = df_model)
obs_df <- data.frame(
  times = times_seq,
  CIF_obs = predict(ci_fit, cause = 1, times = times_seq)
)

plot_df <- left_join(obs_df, pred_df, by = "times")

# ============================================================
# 4. Dot dataset with event type
# ============================================================
dot_df <- df_model %>%
  mutate(
    dot_type = case_when(
      outcome_comp == 1 ~ "CRE infection",
      outcome_comp == 2 ~ "Discharge",
      outcome_comp == 3 ~ "Death",
      outcome_comp == 0 ~ "Censored"
    ),
    # assign strip positions
    y_pos = case_when(
      outcome_comp == 1 ~ -0.01,   # infections (bottom)
      outcome_comp == 2 ~ 0.26,    # discharge
      outcome_comp == 3 ~ 0.27,    # death
      outcome_comp == 0 ~ 0.28     # censored
    )
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(dot_type))

# ============================================================
# 5. NEJM-style plot with jittered dots + legend
# ============================================================
nejm_colors <- c("Observed" = "black",
                 "Predicted" = "#D81B60")

dot_colors <- c("CRE infection" = "red",
                "Discharge"    = "green3",
                "Death"        = "black",
                "Censored"     = "grey50")

p_calib <- ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = times)) +
  # Calibration curves
  geom_line(aes(y = CIF_obs, color = "Observed"), size = 1.2) +
  geom_line(aes(y = CIF_pred, color = "Predicted"),
            size = 1.2, linetype = "dashed") +
  # Jittered dots with legend
  geom_jitter(
    data = dot_df,
    aes(x = time_c, y = y_pos, color = dot_type),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    width = 0, height = 0.002,
    shape = 16, alpha = 0.6, size = 1.5
  ) +
  labs(x = "Days since OLT",
       y = "Cumulative incidence of CRE infection",
       title = "Observed vs Predicted CIF with Patient Distributions") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(nejm_colors, dot_colors)) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, max(plot_df$CIF_obs, plot_df$CIF_pred, na.rm = TRUE) * 1.1)) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "top",
    legend.title = element_blank(),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 11),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 12),
    axis.ticks.length = unit(0.2, "cm")
  )

print(p_calib)















Figure 2: Calibration plot of CRECOOLT risk model in total combined population



The calibration of the CRECOOLT prediction model stratified by retrospective versus prospectively enrolled patients in shown in Figure 3.

Code
#############################################
## Stratified calibration with separated outcome dots
#############################################

library(dplyr)
library(riskRegression)
library(prodlim)
library(ggplot2)

## 1) Prep with study design
df_model <- data_extract %>%
  mutate(
    post_olt_compli1 = as.integer(post_olt_compli___1 == 1),
    post_olt_compli3 = as.integer(post_olt_compli___3 == 1),
    post_olt_compli5 = as.integer(post_olt_compli___5 == 1),
    multisite_col    = as.integer(multisite_colonization == 1),
    cre_col1         = as.integer(cre_colonization___1 == 1),
    cre_col2         = as.integer(cre_colonization___2 == 1),
    retro_group      = factor(ifelse(retro_or_pros == 1, "Retrospective", "Prospective"))
  ) %>%
  select(time_c, outcome_comp, retro_group,
         post_olt_compli1, post_olt_compli3, post_olt_compli5,
         multisite_col, cre_col1, cre_col2) %>%
  filter(!is.na(time_c) & time_c >= 0 & !is.na(retro_group))

## 2) Fit FG once on full data
fg_risk <- FGR(
  Hist(time_c, outcome_comp) ~ 
    post_olt_compli1 + post_olt_compli3 + post_olt_compli5 +
    multisite_col + cre_col1 + cre_col2,
  data  = df_model,
  cause = 1
)

## 3) Time grid
times_seq <- seq(0, 180, by = 5)

## 4) Helper to get obs + pred per subgroup
get_obs_pred <- function(dat, group_label){
  if (nrow(dat) < 2) return(NULL)
  ci_fit <- prodlim(Hist(time_c, outcome_comp) ~ 1, data = dat)
  obs_df <- data.frame(
    times   = times_seq,
    CIF_obs = predict(ci_fit, cause = 1, times = times_seq)
  )
  pred_cif <- predict(
    fg_risk, newdata = dat, cause = 1, times = times_seq,
    se = FALSE, keep.newdata = FALSE
  )
  pred_df <- data.frame(
    times    = times_seq,
    CIF_pred = colMeans(pred_cif, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
  dplyr::left_join(obs_df, pred_df, by = "times") %>%
    mutate(group = group_label)
}

## 5) Build calibration data for facets
plot_df <- bind_rows(
  get_obs_pred(filter(df_model, retro_group == "Retrospective"), "Retrospective"),
  get_obs_pred(filter(df_model, retro_group == "Prospective"),  "Prospective")
)

## --- Dynamic strip positions so dots never get clipped ----
y_max <- max(plot_df$CIF_obs, plot_df$CIF_pred, na.rm = TRUE)
y_bottom  <- -0.03 * y_max                  # CRE dots (bottom)
y_top_base <-  1.02 * y_max                 # base just above curves
y_discharge <- y_top_base + 0.01 * y_max
y_death     <- y_top_base + 0.03 * y_max
y_censored  <- y_top_base + 0.05 * y_max
y_min_plot  <- y_bottom - 0.01 * y_max
y_max_plot  <- y_top_base + 0.07 * y_max

## 6) Dot data (with facet variable)
dot_df <- df_model %>%
  mutate(
    group   = retro_group,  # match facet variable
    dot_type = case_when(
      outcome_comp == 1 ~ "CRE infection",
      outcome_comp == 2 ~ "Discharge",
      outcome_comp == 3 ~ "Death",
      outcome_comp == 0 ~ "Censored",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    y_pos = case_when(
      outcome_comp == 1 ~ y_bottom,
      outcome_comp == 2 ~ y_discharge,
      outcome_comp == 3 ~ y_death,
      outcome_comp == 0 ~ y_censored,
      TRUE ~ NA_real_
    )
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(dot_type))

## 7) Plot
nejm_colors <- c("Observed" = "black",
                 "Predicted" = "#D81B60")

dot_colors <- c("CRE infection" = "red",
                "Discharge"    = "green3",
                "Death"        = "black",
                "Censored"     = "grey50")

p_calib_strat <- ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = times)) +
  # curves
  geom_line(aes(y = CIF_obs, color = "Observed"), size = 1.2) +
  geom_line(aes(y = CIF_pred, color = "Predicted"),
            size = 1.2, linetype = "dashed") +
  # dots (with facet-aware data)
  geom_jitter(
    data = dot_df,
    aes(x = time_c, y = y_pos, color = dot_type),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    width = 0, height = 0.002,
    shape = 16,
    alpha = 0.7,
    size = ifelse(dot_df$dot_type == "Death", 1.9, 1.4)  # make deaths a touch larger
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~group) +
  labs(x = "Days since OLT",
       y = "Cumulative incidence of CRE infection",
       title = "Observed vs Predicted CIF by Study Design") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(nejm_colors, dot_colors)) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(y_min_plot, y_max_plot)) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "top",
    legend.title = element_blank(),
    legend.text  = element_text(size = 11),
    axis.title   = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
    axis.text    = element_text(size = 12),
    axis.ticks.length = unit(0.2, "cm")
  )

print(p_calib_strat)















Figure 3: Calibration plot of CRECOOLT risk model stratified by prospectively vs. retrospectively enrolled patients



The distribution of model covariates differed somewhat in the retrospective versus prospective groups as shown in Figure 4.

Code
#############################################
## Bar plot of covariate distribution
## Retrospective vs Prospective cohorts
## with custom labels
#############################################

library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)

# ============================================================
# 1. Select the covariates of interest
# ============================================================
covariates <- c("post_olt_compli1", "post_olt_compli3", "post_olt_compli5",
                "multisite_col", "cre_col1", "cre_col2")

df_cov <- df_model %>%
  select(retro_group, all_of(covariates))

# ============================================================
# 2. Reshape to long format
# ============================================================
df_long <- df_cov %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = all_of(covariates),
    names_to = "covariate",
    values_to = "value"
  )

# ============================================================
# 3. Summarize % positive per group
# ============================================================
df_summary <- df_long %>%
  group_by(retro_group, covariate) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    n_pos = sum(value == 1, na.rm = TRUE),
    pct_pos = 100 * n_pos / n,
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    covariate_label = recode(covariate,
                             "cre_col1"          = "Prior",
                             "cre_col2"          = "Post",
                             "mec1"              = "KPC",
                             "multisite_col"     = "Multisite",
                             "post_olt_compli1"  = "ARF",
                             "post_olt_compli3"  = "Vent",
                             "post_olt_compli5"  = "Reint"
    )
  )

# ============================================================
# 4. Bar plot (NEJM-style)
# ============================================================
p_cov <- ggplot(df_summary, aes(x = covariate_label, y = pct_pos, fill = retro_group)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.7) +
  labs(x = "Covariate", y = "Percent positive (%)",
       title = "Distribution of Covariates by Study Design") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Retrospective" = "#1E88E5", "Prospective" = "#D81B60")) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1, size = 11),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "top",
    legend.title = element_blank()
  )

print(p_cov)















Figure 4: Distribution of CRECOOLT model covariates in the prospective vs. retrospective cohorts



This distribution can be contrasted with the initial variable distributions from the CRECOOLT 2.0 study1 as shown in Figure 5.

Code
#############################################
## Bar plot of covariate distribution
## Previous study (CRE3 dataset)
#############################################

library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)

# ============================================================
# 1. Load and clean CRE3 dataset
# ============================================================
CRE3 <- read.csv("CRE3.csv", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

CRE3_clean <- CRE3 %>%
  mutate(
    time        = as.numeric(time),
    status      = as.numeric(status),
    reint       = as.numeric(reint),
    mv          = as.numeric(mv),
    arf         = as.numeric(arf),
    crepre_60   = as.numeric(crepre_60),
    crepost_60  = as.numeric(crepost_60),
    multipost   = as.numeric(multipost)
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(time), !is.na(status), time > 0)

# ============================================================
# 2. Select predictors and reshape to long format
# ============================================================
predictors <- c("reint", "mv", "arf", "crepre_60", "crepost_60", "multipost")

df_long <- CRE3_clean %>%
  select(all_of(predictors)) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = all_of(predictors),
    names_to = "covariate",
    values_to = "value"
  )

# ============================================================
# 3. Summarize % positive for each covariate
# ============================================================
df_summary <- df_long %>%
  group_by(covariate) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    n_pos = sum(value == 1, na.rm = TRUE),
    pct_pos = 100 * n_pos / n,
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    covariate_label = recode(covariate,
      "reint"       = "Reint",
      "mv"          = "Vent",
      "arf"         = "ARF",
      "crepre_60"   = "Coln prior",
      "crepost_60"  = "Coln post",
      "multipost"   = "Multisite"
    )
  )

# ============================================================
# 4. Bar plot (NEJM-style)
# ============================================================
p_cov_CRE3 <- ggplot(df_summary, aes(x = covariate_label, y = pct_pos)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#1E88E5", width = 0.7) +
  labs(x = "Covariate", y = "Percent positive (%)",
       title = "Covariate Distribution (Previous Study, CRE3)") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1, size = 11),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank()
  )

print(p_cov_CRE3)















Figure 5: Distribution of covariates in the original CRECOOLT 2.0 model









ImportantInteresting observation



There is a marked difference in the pre OLT versus post OLT CRE colonization rates between the previous CRECOOLT cohort and the current study. Most of the patients in the previous study had CRE colonization after OLT whereas in the current cohorts CRE colonization occurred before OLT. Yet the model still seemed to predict the risk of CRE infection well.






References

1. Giannella, M. et al. Development of a risk prediction model for carbapenem-resistant enterobacteriaceae infection after liver transplantation: A multinational cohort study. Clinical Infectious Diseases 73, E955–E966 (2021).

SCORE TRAJECTORY ANALYSIS - PROSPECTIVE ONLY


Simplified Approach

Final longitudinal records: 649

=== Trajectory Analysis ===

Patient trajectory summary:
  Patients analyzed: 158 
  Mean scores per patient: 4.11 











Individual patient score trajectories (gray lines) and locally weighted regression curves (LOESS, red line with 95% confidence interval in gray shading) are shown for the first 75 days post-transplant, stratified by primary clinical outcome. The risk score incorporates clinical and laboratory parameters to quantify CRE infection risk. Censored patients (n=X) had incomplete follow-up without reaching a definitive endpoint. CRE infection (n=X) includes all patients who developed carbapenem-resistant Enterobacteriaceae infections during follow-up. Death (n=X) represents all-cause mortality. Discharge (n=X) indicates successful hospital discharge without CRE infection or death. Score trajectories demonstrate distinct patterns: discharged patients show early peak and resolution, deaths show progressive elevation, CRE infections display high variability, and censored patients maintain persistently elevated scores.

title: CRE Infection Risk and Mortality in Liver Transplant Patients
text: This report analyzes data from 692 liver transplant patients in the CRECOOLT study. Key findings: CRE infection increases mortality risk by 3.6-fold - 71% of CRE infections occur within the first month post-transplant - Respiratory infections have the highest mortality (~42%) - Surgical/intra-abdominal infections (SSI and IAI merged) account for 37% mortality - Infection type matters more than baseline patient characteristics for predicting outcomes

library(survminer)
library(patchwork)

# Load data
CRECOOLT_overall <- read_csv("CRECOOLT_overall.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

# Create baseline dataset (non-repeated instruments)
baseline_data <- CRECOOLT_overall |>
  filter(is.na(redcap_repeat_instrument))

# Basic summary
n_patients <- nrow(baseline_data)
n_variables <- ncol(CRECOOLT_overall)


The CRECOOLT dataset contains 692 unique patients with 310 variables tracking:

Demographics and comorbidities
Transplant characteristics
CRE colonization and infection data
Antibiotic resistance patterns
Clinical outcomes and mortality

text:
Survival Analysis

Kaplan-Meier Survival Curves


Code
# Prepare survival data
surv_data <- baseline_data |>
  filter(!is.na(cre_infection), !is.na(death)) |>
  mutate(
    cre_status = factor(
      ifelse(cre_infection == 1, "CRE Infection", "No CRE Infection"),
      levels = c("No CRE Infection", "CRE Infection")
    ),
    time = ifelse(!is.na(OLT_to_death), OLT_to_death, 
                  ifelse(!is.na(LOS), LOS, NA)),
    event = death
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(time), time > 0)

# Fit survival curves
surv_obj <- Surv(time = surv_data$time, event = surv_data$event)
surv_fit <- survfit(surv_obj ~ cre_status, data = surv_data)

# Plot with 360-day truncation
ggsurvplot(
  surv_fit,
  data = surv_data,
  pval = TRUE,
  conf.int = TRUE,
  risk.table = TRUE,
  risk.table.height = 0.25,
  xlab = "Time from Transplant (days)",
  ylab = "Survival Probability",
  title = "Survival Curves by CRE Infection Status",
  subtitle = "Kaplan-Meier estimates with 95% CI (1-year follow-up)",
  legend.labs = c("No CRE Infection", "CRE Infection"),
  palette = c("#2E7D32", "#C62828"),
  xlim = c(0, 360),
  break.x.by = 60,
  ggtheme = theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
)











Survival at Key Timepoints


Code
# Calculate survival at specific time points
time_points <- c(30, 90, 180)
surv_summary <- summary(surv_fit, times = time_points)

surv_rates <- data.frame(
  time = surv_summary$time,
  group = rep(c("No CRE Infection", "CRE Infection"), each = length(time_points)),
  survival_pct = round(surv_summary$surv * 100, 1),
  mortality_pct = round((1 - surv_summary$surv) * 100, 1)
) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = group, values_from = c(survival_pct, mortality_pct))

title: CRE Infection Risk and Mortality in Liver Transplant Patients
text:
Infection Source Analysis

Infection Type Descriptions
The following CRE infection types were recorded in this cohort:







Infection Type
Description




Bloodstream (BSI)
CRE isolated from ≥1 blood culture, with or without an identified primary source. Includes primary and secondary bacteraemias.


Respiratory
CRE isolated from respiratory specimens (bronchoalveolar lavage, tracheal aspirate, or sputum) with compatible clinical signs of pneumonia or tracheobronchitis.


Urinary (UTI)
CRE isolated from urine culture with clinical signs of urinary tract infection. Includes both lower and upper urinary tract infections.


Surgical/Intra-abdominal
CRE isolated from intra-abdominal collections, surgical drains, bile, wound cultures, or peritoneal fluid after liver transplantation. Encompasses both surgical site infections (SSI) and intra-abdominal infections (IAI), which were combined due to overlapping clinical presentation and inconsistency in the original coding (see note below).


Other
CRE infections not fitting the above categories (e.g., central line-associated, skin/soft tissue).









WarningData Quality Note: SSI and IAI Coding



The original dataset recorded surgical site infections (SSI, infection_source___5) and intra-abdominal infections (IAI, infection_source___4) as separate categories. However, two anomalies suggest possible data entry errors:

Implausible mortality discrepancy: SSI showed 46.9% mortality vs 14.3% for IAI — an unusually large gap for two closely related infection foci in the same anatomical region after liver transplantation.
Negative time-to-infection in the IAI group: Some IAI patients had OLT_to_CREinfection values below zero (minimum: −265 days), indicating the infection was recorded as occurring before transplantation, which is implausible.

These findings are consistent with miscoding between SSI and IAI in a subset of patients. The two categories have therefore been merged into a single “Surgical/Intra-abdominal” category for all source-level analyses. No patient had both SSI and IAI coded simultaneously, so there is no double-counting.




Mortality by Infection Source


Code
# Prepare infection source data
# SSI (infection_source___5) and IAI (infection_source___4) are merged into a
# single "Surgical/Intra-abdominal" category due to probable data entry errors
# (see Data Quality Note above).
infection_source_data <- baseline_data |>
  filter(cre_infection == 1) |>
  select(record_id, death, mortality_90day, starts_with("infection_source___")) |>
  mutate(
    bloodstream           = ifelse(infection_source___1 == 1, 1, 0),
    respiratory           = ifelse(infection_source___2 == 1, 1, 0),
    urinary               = ifelse(infection_source___3 == 1, 1, 0),
    surgical_abdominal    = ifelse(infection_source___4 == 1 |
                                   infection_source___5 == 1, 1, 0),
    other                 = ifelse(infection_source___6 == 1, 1, 0)
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(death))

# Calculate mortality for each source
sources_list <- list(
  "Bloodstream (BSI)"          = "bloodstream",
  "Respiratory"                = "respiratory",
  "Urinary (UTI)"              = "urinary",
  "Surgical/Intra-abdominal"   = "surgical_abdominal",
  "Other"                      = "other"
)

mortality_by_source <- map_df(names(sources_list), function(source_name) {
  source_col <- sources_list[[source_name]]
  source_subset <- infection_source_data |>
    filter(get(source_col) == 1)
  
  if (nrow(source_subset) > 0) {
    data.frame(
      Source = source_name,
      n_infections = nrow(source_subset),
      deaths = sum(source_subset$death, na.rm = TRUE),
      mortality_rate = round(sum(source_subset$death, na.rm = TRUE) / 
                            nrow(source_subset) * 100, 1),
      deaths_90d = sum(source_subset$mortality_90day, na.rm = TRUE),
      mortality_90d_rate = round(sum(source_subset$mortality_90day, na.rm = TRUE) / 
                                nrow(source_subset) * 100, 1)
    )
  }
}) |>
  arrange(desc(mortality_rate))

highest_mort_source <- mortality_by_source$Source[1]
highest_mort <- mortality_by_source$mortality_rate[1]
lowest_mort_source <- mortality_by_source$Source[nrow(mortality_by_source)]
lowest_mort <- mortality_by_source$mortality_rate[nrow(mortality_by_source)]

Respiratory infections show a delayed mortality pattern (42.4% overall, but only 18.2% at 90 days), suggesting late deterioration
